introHtml: Web performance is a first-class interview topic, especially for senior roles. Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP) and their measurement, the critical rendering path, bundle optimization, lazy loading, and service workers are all fair game. Always connect performance work to user impact and business outcomes.

topics.bodyHtml:
MetricMeasuresGoodNeeds WorkPoor
LCP (Largest Contentful Paint)Loading — when largest element renders< 2.5s2.5s–4s> 4s
INP (Interaction to Next Paint)Responsiveness — worst interaction latency< 200ms200–500ms> 500ms
CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift)Stability — unexpected visual shifts< 0.10.1–0.25> 0.25

Improving LCP

  • Preload the hero image: <link rel="preload" as="image" href="hero.webp" fetchpriority="high">
  • Do NOT lazy-load above-the-fold images
  • Use CDN, eliminate render-blocking resources
  • Serve images in WebP/AVIF format (30-50% smaller)
  • Use fetchpriority="high" on LCP image element

Fixing CLS

  • Always set width and height on images (browser reserves space)
  • Reserve space for ads/embeds with aspect-ratio or min-height
  • Avoid inserting content above existing content after load
  • Use font-display: optional to avoid layout shift from font swap

Fixing INP

  • Break up long tasks (over 50ms) with setTimeout or scheduler.yield()
  • Move non-UI work to Web Workers
  • Use startTransition in React for non-urgent updates
  • Avoid blocking the main thread during input events

// Critical Rendering Path:
// HTML parse -> DOM + CSSOM -> Render Tree -> Layout -> Paint -> Composite

// CSS always render-blocking (blocks paint until parsed)
// Inline critical CSS for above-the-fold content
// <link rel="stylesheet" media="print" onload="this.media='all'"> for non-critical

// Script loading comparison:
// <script src="app.js">          — blocks HTML parsing AND rendering
// <script defer src="app.js">    — downloads in parallel, executes after DOM, in order
// <script async src="app.js">    — downloads in parallel, executes immediately when done (no order guarantee)
// <script type="module">         — deferred by default + strict mode

// Resource hints
<!-- DNS + TCP + TLS pre-setup -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
<!-- DNS only -->
<link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://analytics.example.com">
<!-- High-priority fetch (fonts, hero image) -->
<link rel="preload" as="font" href="font.woff2" crossorigin>
<link rel="preload" as="image" href="hero.webp">
<!-- Idle-time fetch for next navigation -->
<link rel="prefetch" href="/next-page-bundle.js">

// Code splitting: dynamic imports create separate chunks
const LargeChart = React.lazy(() => import("./LargeChart"));
// Route-level splitting with React Router v6
const routes = [
  { path: "/", lazy: () => import("./pages/Home") },
  { path: "/analytics", lazy: () => import("./pages/Analytics") }
];

// Tree shaking: dead code elimination
// Requires ES modules + "sideEffects": false in package.json
import { debounce } from "lodash-es";  // tree-shakeable

// Responsive images
<img
  src="image-800.webp"
  srcset="image-400.webp 400w, image-800.webp 800w, image-1200.webp 1200w"
  sizes="(max-width: 600px) 100vw, 800px"
  loading="lazy"
  decoding="async"
  width="800"
  height="450"
/>

// HTTP Caching strategy
// Versioned assets (content hash in filename): immutable, long cache
// app.abc123.js  ->  Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable
// HTML files     ->  Cache-Control: no-cache (revalidate, short TTL)
// API data       ->  depends on endpoint (no-store for sensitive, short max-age for data)

// Service Worker: intercept network requests, offline caching
self.addEventListener("install", event => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.open("v1").then(cache => cache.addAll(["/", "/app.js", "/style.css"]))
  );
});

self.addEventListener("fetch", event => {
  event.respondWith(
    caches.match(event.request).then(cached => {
      const network = fetch(event.request).then(response => {
        caches.open("v1").then(c => c.put(event.request, response.clone()));
        return response;
      });
      return cached || network;  // cache-first
    })
  );
});

// Web Worker: off-main-thread computation
const worker = new Worker("worker.js", { type: "module" });
worker.postMessage({ type: "SORT", data: hugeArray });
worker.onmessage = ({ data }) => displaySortedResults(data);

// worker.js
self.onmessage = ({ data }) => {
  if (data.type === "SORT") {
    const sorted = data.data.sort((a, b) => a - b);
    self.postMessage(sorted);
  }
};

// SharedArrayBuffer: zero-copy memory sharing with workers
// Requires: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy + Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy headers

items.question: What do LCP, CLS, and INP measure, and why should frontend engineers care?
items.answerHtml: LCP measures how quickly the main visible content appears, CLS measures unexpected layout movement, and INP measures overall responsiveness to user interactions. They matter because they map performance to what users actually feel rather than just bundle size or synthetic lab metrics.
items.flashcardBackHtml: LCP is loading speed of main content, CLS is visual stability, and INP is interaction responsiveness.

items.question: What is the critical rendering path in the browser?
items.answerHtml: It is the sequence from HTML download through parsing, CSSOM and DOM creation, render tree construction, layout, paint, and compositing. Scripts and blocking stylesheets can delay this path, which is why loading strategy matters so much.
items.flashcardBackHtml: It is the browser pipeline that turns downloaded resources into pixels on the screen.

items.question: Why can reducing JavaScript bundle size improve more than just download time?
items.answerHtml: Smaller bundles also reduce parse, compile, and execution cost on the main thread. On slower devices this CPU cost is often as important as network transfer time, so code splitting and tree shaking can improve both loading and interactivity.

items.question: A page looks visually complete quickly but remains hard to interact with for several seconds. What would you investigate?
items.answerHtml: I would inspect main-thread work after paint: hydration cost, large bundles, expensive effects, synchronous data transforms, and long tasks. This is a classic gap between perceived loading and actual interactivity.

items.question: How would you decide between a service worker and a web worker when someone says the app is slow?
items.answerHtml: Use a service worker for network-related concerns such as caching, offline support, and request interception. Use a web worker for CPU-heavy computation that should move off the main thread. They solve very different classes of performance problems.

items.question: Images dominate payload size on a landing page. What optimizations would you mention first?
items.answerHtml: I would mention modern formats, responsive image sizing, lazy loading below the fold, CDN delivery, compression, avoiding oversized hero assets, and preloading only the truly critical image. Strong answers tie each tactic to user-visible metrics rather than naming tools randomly.

keyTakeaways.title: ⚡ Key Takeaways
itemsHtml: LCP measures loading (target <2.5s), CLS measures visual stability (target <0.1), INP measures responsiveness (target <200ms)
Preload critical resources (<link rel="preload">); defer non-critical JavaScript (<script defer>)
Code splitting and lazy loading reduce initial bundle size; React.lazy and dynamic import() are the standard tools
Images should use WebP/AVIF, responsive srcset, and explicit width/height attributes to prevent CLS
Service workers enable offline support and instant repeat visits via cache-first strategies
Web Workers offload CPU-intensive work (parsing, encryption) off the main thread to prevent jank
